data.title: RVT: Small-Cap Stocks Poised To Outperform In 2026
data.description:
(Note: This article was originally published exclusively for members of the Inside the Income Factory investing group.)

As a now retired income investor, I follow the macro events that dictate which investments may perform better at various times throughout the economic cycle. Over the past two to three years, large-cap tech stocks have been leading the way higher for US-based stocks. The Magnificent Seven (MAGS) stocks have been driving equity returns due in large part to the AI rally that has driven exponential growth in the large-cap tech sector. But now in early 2026, investors are looking to rotate out of large caps, and one area that looks promising this year is the small-cap equity asset class. According to Columbia Threadneedle Investments:

With so much attention on the “Magnificent Seven,” many investors are looking for opportunities in other parts of the market. Our forecast suggests that U.S. small-cap stocks will outperform large caps over the next several years. Two dynamics, in particular, make this an area to watch:

  • Interest rate sensitivity: Lower interest rates typically benefit small-cap companies more than their large-cap counterparts. Smaller firms often rely on borrowing to fuel growth. Lower financing rates make that debt easier to service and new projects more profitable.
  • Improved market breadth: We see improving market breadth, measured by the percentage of Russell 3000 companies trading above their 200-day moving average. With more stocks benefiting from positive momentum, we are more likely to see a rally that extends beyond just a few key mega-cap names. We believe this momentum has room to run, given the higher levels of breadth we observed in 2021.

As an income investor looking to capitalize on this macro trend, I am looking to add shares of a closed-end fund that has been around for nearly forty years and has delivered market-beating returns over the past ten years and since inception with its small-cap equity holdings. That CEF is the Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), initially launched in 1986 by Charles (Chuck) Royce as the Royce Value Trust. Chuck Royce will be 86 in 2026 and these days has a whole team managing the fund, which is one of several investment funds offered by Royce Investment Partners, now a Franklin Templeton company.

Over the past ten years, RVT has delivered consistently strong total returns (with dividends reinvested) and has at times beaten the S&P 500.

Although the fund benchmark is the Russell 2000 and the fund’s investment objective is to outperform the benchmark, which it did for all time periods measured as of 12/31/25 (from the fund website).

The Income Compounding Effect

One of the best ways to illustrate the power of my Income Compounder approach of reinvesting dividends (modeled after Steven Bavaria’s Income Factory) into more shares of income funds like RVT is this chart showing the growth in the value of a $10,000 investment made at fund inception, nearly 40 years ago. That investment as of 9/30/25 would be worth $479,323, and even more than that today!

Another way to look at this income compounding effect is to compare RVT performance with a fund that tracks its benchmark, iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), which pays minimal dividends and relies almost exclusively on capital gains. As you can see, RVT delivered a total return of 255% over the past ten years compared to 190% for IWM.

RVT Fund Basics

One thing to note regarding RVT that makes it stand out as a Buy in January 2026 is the fact that it trades at a discount to NAV of -9% (despite a rising NAV). Another is the current yield shown of 13%, which is somewhat distorted due to a larger than typical fourth quarter dividend paid in 2025. Assuming the more typical quarterly dividends of roughly $0.30 per share going forward in 2026, the yield would be approximately 7% based on the closing market price of $16.74 as of 1/8/26.

The fund holdings include what Morningstar classifies as Small-Cap Core holdings, about halfway between growth and value, as of June 30, 2025, as shown on the fact sheet.

Those holdings included 467 equity positions as of 9/30/25, with top allocations to Industrials at 25% and Financials at 21% of total portfolio value, also shown on the fund fact sheet.

The top ten holdings represent less than 12% of the total portfolio, which is an indication that the portfolio is diversified and not subject to much concentration risk if, for example, something bad happened to top holding IES Holdings, Inc. (IES).

Why Small-Cap Stocks in 2026?

According to this very detailed insight into the outlook for small caps in 2026, Royce portfolio managers offer their comments, which are mostly optimistic:

Miles Lewis: We believe that both small-cap quality and value are poised for meaningful rebounds in 2026. 2025’s returns, particularly since the April lows, have been driven primarily by lower quality, speculative stocks and just about anything that is an obvious beneficiary of the AI boom … Furthermore, more “traditional” businesses models—those that have healthy margins, generate free cash flow, grow modestly, and have strong, self-funding balance sheets that also trade at attractive valuations (i.e., quality value stocks) should recapture the interest of investors as the junk rally fizzles.

Chip Skinner: We are constructive on small cap growth stocks as we head into 2026, primarily due to the accommodative fiscal and monetary policies of the current administration. The speculative activity that characterized the early part of the fourth quarter has since faded, and we are encouraged by a broadening in positive earnings revisions as macro headwinds continue to ease.

A key—and in our view underappreciated—tailwind entering 2026 is the acceleration of fiscal spending tied to onshoring initiatives, industrial policy, infrastructure, and energy-related programs. Coupled with an expected Fed easing cycle over the coming months, these forces should support meaningful economic expansion and create a favorable backdrop for small-cap growth companies. Many of these businesses have spent the past few years improving cost structures and sharpening execution, positioning them to deliver strong operating leverage as demand reaccelerates in 2026.

Kavitha Venkatraman: Regardless of one’s politics, we think the “Big Beautiful Bill” will prove highly stimulative to small-cap companies and to lower-end consumers in 2026. The 100% bonus depreciation for certain capital investments and immediate expensing of R&D spend—as opposed to it being amortized over several years—are both attractive features of the bill, particularly for smaller companies. These new rules incentivize businesses to redirect their saved tax dollars to productive uses, which is powerful for smaller businesses.

Steven McBoyle: I expect U.S. small-cap Industrials—particularly precision manufacturers, engineered components suppliers, and value-added industrial technology providers—to perform well in 2026. We are already seeing increased activity in select areas, such as improving order books across specialty manufacturing, an improved aerospace supply chain, and growth in automation/controls applications. Against this favorable backdrop, operating leverage appears poised to expand as supply chains normalize and freight and input costs stabilize.

Francis Gannon: The Russell 2000 Index is up more than 45% since the market’s low on 4/8/25, a span that has also seen small-caps beat their large-cap counterparts. In light of this dynamic performance, it may seem counterintuitive that my outlook has not shifted much since the end of the second quarter. Even with these robust results, however, small-cap stocks as a group remain far more attractively valued than their large- and mega-cap peers, as measured by our preferred index valuation metric, EV/EBIT—enterprise value over earnings before interest & taxes. The same holds true for micro-caps—which have rebounded even more impressively since early April, up more than 68%—relative to large-cap stocks.

Risks to Small-Cap Performance

One of the things that has been a tailwind for small-cap stocks in 2025 was the latest Fed rate cut and a continuing consensus belief in at least one to two additional rate cuts in 2026. If inflation starts to increase again as the impact from tariffs wears off (or the Supreme Court rules against them), the rate cut talk might be cut short and the small-cap rally will be interrupted again.

The shift from the AI-frenzied rally in growth stocks could also lose steam quickly and switch to a broader fear in the market as valuations suddenly appear excessive even for those small-cap value stocks that have been improving their balance sheets. When the tide goes out, all boats (stocks) will be affected, even the ones that have been undervalued relative to large caps that have been booming.

Conclusion: RVT is Worth Buying and Should Perform Well in 2026

The comment below from Royce Managing Director Francis Gannon sums up the current environment and expected outcome for small-cap stocks, making the case for a Buy rating on RVT, which I agree with:

So, while a fair amount of uncertainty exists in the U.S. economy and on the geopolitical front, our investment teams are highly confident that small-cap can sustain, if not build on, its nascent market leadership. Finally, I would remind investors that the opportunity still exists to build one’s small-cap allocation at attractive valuations. We continue to see the current period as an opportune time to invest in select small-caps for the long run.

In other words, even though small caps have begun to take over the leadership position from large-cap stocks in early 2026, there is still plenty of upside ahead, especially for long-term income investors who wish to compound their income by reinvesting dividends. If you do not already have an allocation to small-cap stocks in your income portfolio, consider RVT while it still trades at a wide discount to NAV.

data.title: Airship AI: Backlog Helps, But Timing Still Rules
data.description:
Q3 FY25 was another reminder of how volatile Airship AI Holdings, Inc.'s (AISP) business is. It is still primarily a procurement-driven story, one where revenue was lower year over year, cash burned faster than I would like, and the overall results depended more on contract awards and shipment timing than on the company's execution.

The stock is down another ~40% from my October article, but I don't think that is a sign for us to dismiss what is happening in the background. The company has a solid backlog, along with a large deal pipeline and recent federal awards, so it is gaining traction with the right customers. I discussed the warrant exercise in October, too.

Now, I want to assess the company's performance, whether it is any closer to being a bit more consistent, and whether the current setup justifies more patience or just caution.

Q3 2025 In Review

  • Revenue: $1.18M (vs. $2.87M YoY).

  • Gross profit: $0.60M.

  • Gross margin: ~51%.

  • Operating loss: $(2.87)M.

  • Net income: $6.4M (driven by non-cash fair value gains).

Year-to-date (FY25: The nine months ended Sept. 30):

  • Revenue: $8.83M (vs. $19.84M YoY).

  • Operating loss: $(6.60)M.

Balance sheet (end of Q3):

  • Cash: $5.76M.

  • Deferred revenue: ~$7.4M.

  • Backlog: ~$11M (shipping Q4 2025 into early 2026).

  • Validated pipeline: ~$166M (18-24 month conversion window).

I can't say that Q3 FY25 was a good-looking quarter, but after Q2's performance, I'm not surprised. The large federal purchase orders that came in Q3 FY24 didn't repeat this time, and that's a blow when most of your revenue is recognized in-quarter and depends on shipment timing.

Besides that, the gross margin was just above 50%, which is 20+ percentage points lower on both a sequential and YoY basis. Of course, I understand that the margins are lower because the company sold a heavier mix of third-party hardware, but that buttresses my point about the company still selling solutions. It cannot yet rely solely on pure software sales, which is where the higher margins are.

However, you wouldn't quite know that the results weren't great if you just looked at the net income figure of $6.4 million. The company's operating loss for Q3 came out to $2.9 million, but it also recorded $9.3 million in non-cash gains related to its warrant and earnout liabilities. That's what gave it the net income boost. It has way less cash now than it did at the beginning of FY25, so it makes sense that it needed the warrant exercise after Q3 ended.

I'm not writing it off, though, because I'm considering the size of the order book here. The company has a backlog of around $11 million, and its validated pipeline amounts to $166 million. Those figures are evidence that there's a lot of demand for what Airship is selling, so the question now is about its conversion timing and consistency.

The main issue here is that even though it has these tailwinds, delivering on them depends on when customers want their shipments and pay for their orders. It is especially true when you remember that a big part of Airship's sales outlook depends on the US federal government, which is a strategic customer.

These government apparatuses (law enforcement and security agencies have big budgets, long-term needs, and high switching costs once they deploy the systems, but they also have slow and bureaucratic sales cycles. So, it is likely that they push contract awards and shipments, and that affects how and when Airship can recognize its revenue.

I've discussed these in more depth in my previous articles, but the fact is that Airship needs to shift more of its revenue towards software, support, and analytics. That SaaS base comes with recurring revenue, which will give the stock a more solid earnings and multiple profile. Conversely, it must work hard to ensure that converting its backlog is much more predictable than the current rate.

Liquidity & Dilution

Back in October, Airship managed to raise around $9.7 million via a warrant exercise at a price of $4.50 per warrant, so it shouldn't have any cash worries for a while. You can read my full breakdown of the warrant story in the previous coverage. The dilution overhang is still there, given that the stock's current price is around $3.1/share, and now, the future upside is capped by the larger warrant base that will come into play if the stock performs well.

Still, I like that it was non-debt capital, so there are no refinancing risks, covenants, or interest payments for a company that is still trying to execute on and scale its business model. What I want to see next is progress on the revenue side of things because it can't keep relying on capital markets. The runway is longer, yes, but we need Airship to execute and deliver on its potential if it wants to avoid unnecessary future dilutions that can affect investor confidence.

The Stock Is Cheap On Paper

Airship is much cheaper today than it was when I first called it a Buy, which is great if you just want to get in at a lower price. I still believe in the business model and trajectory, but the numbers right now don't inspire confidence just yet. I don't think that any near-term valuation based on "normalized" earnings is fully grounded or justifiable. Long-term, yes, but for now, it's more aspirational, at least until the company has a steadier revenue cadence and we can see a clearer margin structure.

I already laid out my position on the proof points I want to see for Airship to justify more bullishness in the short to medium-term, so it's a waiting game for now.

A Question Of Execution And Consistency

Airship AI is selling products that solve real problems in the right end markets, and it has a big, credible pipeline, but consistency is still the big question. Dilution risk is there too, even though the valuation is cheaper than before. All the same, I can't fully underwrite the upside given the way the last two quarters have gone. I'm waiting for the next few earnings reports to come in so I can gauge Airship's earnings power before re-evaluating my stance.

data.title: CommScope: The Market Is Still Behind The Curve
data.description:
Why Do I Think That COMM Is A Great Value Play Set To Soar Further?

CommScope Holding Company (COMM) is a small-cap firm (~$4.1 billion market cap) that operates in the communications equipment industry of the IT sector, and it's actually ranked quite high on the Quant metrics within this industry.

The business COMM runs is about providing a wide range of network infrastructure solutions, such as fiber optic, coaxial cables, up to data center connectivity to WiFi routers and antennas, and broadband access equipment for cable operators. As another Seeking Alpha analyst, The Curious Analyst wrote in their recent article on COMM, one of the company's unique niches is about supplying the specialized high-performance cables and connectivity for AI supercomputing clusters. So, these aren't ordinary cables - they're designed to handle GPU-to-GPU communications that require "10x more links than traditional server farms."

Right now, CommScope is in a very unique situation with its business structure because it's undergoing a key divestiture - they're going to get rid of their CCS business unit, which contributes over 68% to their consolidated sales (as of Q3). The CCS is growing rapidly, enjoying hyperscalers' data center demand.

In anticipation of this divestiture, COMM almost tripled over the past 12 months, but it remains an interesting value play when we take a look at what's left in store for COMM's investors in the next 12-24 months.

Personally, I see a few catalysts that should play out this year (or next year) and bring in an additional wave of value unlocking.

First off, it's the size of the CCS deal. The expected closing of the CCS segment sale to Amphenol (APH) is going to close in the first half of 2026, according to Seeking Alpha, and CommScope should receive $10.5 billion for its CCS assets from APH. We have to recall that the whole market cap of COMM right now is only $4.1 billion, so COMM will effectively get a massive premium for ~70% of its business, making the transaction "immediately accretive", so to speak. The management was quite straightforward in their plans for the proceeds usage - the majority will go to cover the $7.4 billion in total obligations, and the remaining sum should be enough to redeem all preferred equity capital (~$1.3 billion) plus issue a massive special dividend (~$1.8 billion, $8.20/share). The special dividend - if the planned sale to APH goes through - was calculated by another Seeking Alpha analyst, Steady Quant:

Its total debt is around $7.4B and preferred equity is around $1.3B. According to its plan, after repaying all its debt and redeeming all its preferred equity, the company would have $1.8B excess cash, or $8.2 excess cash per share. Though the company has emphasized that there’s no guarantee about the exact amount of the dividend, we can add this amount of cash per share to our valuation as it’s obviously an additional asset compared to other companies anyway. This yields my final target price of $26.86, provided that the CCS sale can be finalized.

The math looks fair. As I'm writing these lines, COMM has closed at just $18.50/share (Friday, January 9, 2026), so the above special dividend payout implies a one-off yield of 44.32%.

COMM will transform into an entity that consists of its former Access Network Solutions (ANS) and Ruckus Networks businesses, which combined showed a revenue growth of >49% YoY in Q3 2025, and an adjusted EBITDA YoY growth of over 95% in the same period. The consolidated adjusted EBITDA margin of these 2 units was 17.4% in Q3, while the CCS business achieved ~28%, and that might raise concerns for some value investors. But in Q3, COMM spent more than half of its EBIT on interest expenses, which should be gone shortly, so I think RemainCo's EBITDA margin can exceed that of the CCS once the debt is fully repaid (and we have no preferred shares to spend dividends on).

This leads to a preliminary conclusion - COMM's EV/EBITDA should be north of 10-14x once we start to see the post-deleveraging margin expansion effect following the CCS divestment.

Before jumping right to the fair value calculation for COMM, let's try to assess the firm's post-CCS period and what's likely coming for RemainCo's operations in the next 1-2 years.

The second catalyst - this time, it has little to do with EV/EBITDA or any other valuation metric, and the multiple expansion opportunity - lies in the upgrade cycle among cable operators, which are trying to deliver multi-gigabit speeds. CommScope seems to be standing right in the center of this trend with its DOCSIS 4.0 ecosystem, which is sold through its ANS division. As you could have seen in the Q3 highlights from COMM's IR materials above, ANS was actually the fastest-growing segment within COMM's structure, and the reason for that is exactly the record shipment of smart amplifiers and nodes in Q3. And the CEO noted on the call that we're seeing "the early innings of the DOCSIS upgrade".

I would say we're in the early innings of the DOCSIS upgrade. And I think this is a multiyear, several year process to put it in perspective, and we're in the very early innings.

Source: COMM's Q3 2025 earnings call

So, there should be more room for expansion throughout this year and likely in 2027, as COMM integrates tech from the Casa acquisition, having a sustainable pipeline for growth into 2027.

The third point that strongly supports any bullish thesis on COMM is the developments in the enterprise Wi-Fi and edge networking markets. COMM's Ruckus Networks segment has finally returned to double-digit growth after battling channel inventory issues through early 2024, and this comeback aligns well with the ongoing Wi-Fi 7 refresh cycle.

We continue to see strong market conditions driven by the Wi-Fi 7 upgrade cycle. We expect the strong market conditions to remain in 2026. As noted in previous calls, the overhang from channel inventory lasted through the first half of 2024. We are now seeing the benefits of normalized inventory in the channel as well as growing market demand. We continue to drive our go-to-market strategies and RUCKUS new product initiatives.

Source: COMM's Q3 2025 earnings call

From that, I see no major shocks to the demand for COMM's Ruckus Networks offerings, so assuming a few more quarters of solid double-digit revenue growth sounds like a legit idea to me.

With all this in mind, now let's try to value COMM after its stunning 12-month rally.

COMM trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 8.385x, while the median company in its sector trades at 15.6x, according to Seeking Alpha. Such kinds of discounts to the sector's medians can be seen all over the place.

Logically, 2026 is going to be a reset year for COMM after the CCS sales, but given the upcoming deleveraging and preferred stock redemption, the company is still projected to show some modest EPS growth this year, even after the loss of its biggest sales contributor. Where things start to look even more interesting is FY2027, when COMM's EPS should increase by 40%+, leading to a P/E of ~8.6x:

YCharts data shows a consensus FY2026 EBITDA figure of $1.439 billion, with a 10% growth in 2027:

I don't believe these projections, as they seem to miss the CCS sale completely. After the CCS is gone, COMM is likely to close FY2026 at $2.5-3.0 billion in revenues (based on the Q3 IR materials, plus implying a 25-50% YoY growth.) The management guided for FY2025 RemainCo's adjusted EBITDA at $350-375 million, but we have to keep in mind that with DOCSIS 4.0/Wi-Fi 7 cycles driving the new leaner operating structure, there should be a massive uptick in margins and hence the absolute EBITDA figure in FY2026.

Assuming just $2.5 billion in FY2026 sales, and putting a 25% adjusted EBITDA margin on it, I get $625 million for FY2026 EBITDA. After putting an 11x EV/EBITDA on it, I get an implied enterprise value of $6.875 billion. Assuming that there's no debt on the books by the end of fiscal 2026, and there's no minority/preferred capital to adjust on, that's the resulting equity value. In other words, it's a clean market cap target for what COMM equity is trading at right away. So, the upside is ~67% of what COMM is trading at to date, and it's clearly a reason for me to assign the stock a "Buy" rating.

Risks And Final Words

My bullish thesis is heavily dependent on the execution of the upcoming closing of the $10.5 billion CCS sale. In case we see any major delays, the special dividend might be postponed, and some investors might decide to take profits, thus pushing the stock price meaningfully lower from here. Plus, a major part of COMM's past earnings growth was driven by hyperscale AI spending and CCS. So, after the CCS is gone, COMM will have to drive its growth only through ANS and Ruckus, which may turn out to be quite risky if the demand cycles there don't come as I expect.

Despite these risks, I see much more value in COMM than what the market has managed to assign to it so far. I understand why the stock went up so much over the past months, but I see even more upside potential once the simplified business structure and deleveraged balance sheet lead to higher margins.

I rate COMM as a "buy," seeing a 67% upside from today's price.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Coeur Mining: Scaling To A Powerhouse Via The New Gold Merger
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe Coeur Mining (CDE) has a bullish outlook as it evolves from a high-leverage growth company into a 2026 free cash flow story as it moves to integrate the New Gold (NGD) acquisition. The stock’s 200% year-to-date in 2025 has not fully priced in the merger implications. Therefore, the stock is currently a buy for me. The high-grade assets and its diversified hedge provided by New Gold’s copper production give Coeur room to still grow. Therefore, CDE is more of a bet on a senior producer's ability to compound cash than a bet on raw silver prices.

Coeur’s Strategic Assessment

The $7 billion acquisition of New Gold is transformative for Coeur. While the transaction is still subject to a shareholder vote at the end of January, I see the strategic roadmap for the combination as being clear; it’s a shift toward diversified and high-margin production across the seven core operations.

From my perspective, the true value of this merger is the structural change in the company’s revenue mix. By 2026, it is projected to produce 100 million pounds of copper annually, adding a high-margin industrial component that reduces an over-reliance on the silver and gold markets. Investors need to continue monitoring the New Afton extension project; any bottlenecks in this project could significantly reduce the per day throughput.

I also believe the production from the Rochester mines is a critical variable when assessing CDE due to it accounting for 38% of silver production. The company’s 2026 target of 20 million silver ounces requires the facility to maintain its output, which has posed problems in the past. Yet, if it can sustain its output momentum, it will remain a crown jewel of the company’s production.

Integration and technical difficulties are always a threat for mining operations that are aggressively expanding. Yet, management has a strong track record of optimizing its assets. An illustrative point of this strength is the Wharf mine’s performance, returning five times its initial $99.5 million investment. Similarly, the SilverCrest integration has already yielded impressive results; the Las Chispas operation saw its cash flow jump 34% in the third quarter. This track record makes me optimistic that CDE will be able to effectively maximize New Gold’s existing assets.

Coeur’s Financial Assessment

There is a healthy amount of skepticism surrounding Coeur’s ability to nearly triple its EBITDA in twelve months. This would move the company from a projected $1 billion in 2025 to $3 billion in 2026. To me, this EBITDA bridge is built on a structural change from the SilverCrest integration, removing Rochester’s drag from operational hurdles and the smooth integration of its acquisition of New Gold.

A pivotal and under-analyzed component of the company’s future cash flow is its tax positioning. In the third quarter, Coeur recognized a $162 million income tax benefit related to its federal net operating losses. The true takeaway is that this can be a multi-year cash shield. This will likely be the most effective in 2026 as Rochester expands and lets the company retain a higher percentage of operating cash flow.

Another key strength of the company is its rapid deleveraging. By achieving a net cash position and cash balance projected to exceed $500 million, the company is rapidly improving its overall financial health. Changing from debt-fueled expansion to a phase of rising cash flows earns the company the ability to sustain a fairly healthy premium. These changes make it possible for the company to effectively use its $2 billion projected free cash flow for 2026 to continue strengthening the balance sheet and potentially initiating stronger capital return policies.

Weighing Coeur Against Its Peers

Looking at how Coeur stacks up against industry peers, the company is clearly aggressively moving to join the ranks of the premier producers. The deal with New Gold is expected to catapult the company into the top ten of global precious metal companies. The company’s market cap of $12 billion is a far cry from the biggest peers like AngloGold Ashanti (AU) at $46 billion; this valuation gap is what management is aiming to close by scaling the North American footprint.

The company has been enjoying the rally in precious metals, delivering over 220% year-to-date price returns. This performance is very impressive and outpaces rivals like Harmony Gold’s (HMY) 127%. However, the company’s 24-month beta of 1.88 is noticeably higher than the more stable 0.67 beta of AngloGold Ashanti or the 0.19 of Gold Fields (GFI).

If the company is able to meet its 2026 goal of $3 billion in EBITDA and $2 billion in free cash flow, I see it as fundamentally changing the narrative around its current valuation. This influx of cash is able to support its strengthening position in North America; a focus on safer jurisdictions is becoming a difference when compared to peers who have more exposure to higher-risk regions. I see this strategy as helping to encourage a valuation multiple that more closely mirrors its premium rivals.

Determining A Future Stock Valuation

Coeur seems positioned for a major value jump that is driven by the strong profits ahead. Much of this strength is due to the expanding operations at Rochester and the New Gold takeover. These changes allow the company to focus less on big spending projects while consolidating its cash flow.

One big number that drives CDE’s valuation picture is what the merged company will earn when settling into normal output. These EPS estimates for 2026 are $1.44; this is substantially higher than the EPS of $0.83 for 2025. Currently, the company trades at about 14x the expected EPS for 2026. This is significantly below the forward P/E of 24x for 2025.

Assigning even a cautious take on the market’s revaluing of the company as operations ramp-up and precious metals continue to rally could lead to a reasonable return of its P/E to the 16x to 18x range. Using 18x against an estimated $1.44 per share brings its value to $25.92; this suggests there is still upside to the stock as the company continues to consolidate and improve its output.

This growth makes more sense when you see where the business aims to be at $2 billion in usable cash. Once Coeur starts bringing in that level of money, investors may view the shares as less risky. This is a change, alongside its boost in cash available per shareholder, that could open room for better valuations and lead to stronger pricing.

Risks To CDE

Post-Merger Integration Complexity

A large hurdle the company is facing is the need for a seamless integration of the New Gold assets. Managing a transaction of this scale carries major execution risks; specifically, the company must effectively merge the distinct corporate cultures and technical operations with minimal disruptions. This will be a challenge, as the company requires oversight of seven North American operations; a task that could strain management’s resources. Failure to realize the anticipated advantages of synergy between assets and cost savings could cause a downward revision of CDE’s targets.

Geological Complexities

Geological uncertainty is always a threat to mining operations; this fact is particularly true for large-scale projects like New Afton. Additionally, at the Rochester expansion, the ongoing push for production is vulnerable to grade variability, where even minor variations can impact silver output. These risks are also evident in the integration challenges of New Gold’s K-Zone because it depends on constructing new mining levels.

Macroeconomic Sensitivities

The company’s targets are influenced by external factors it cannot control. A clear example is sustained lower market prices for precious metals, as this would compress margins. While Coeur is benefiting from record metal prices, its high beta indicates the stock is in a position to suffer worse pullbacks than some of its peers if there is a commodity downturn. Inflationary pressures also remain a concern because these expenses could outpace the projections management is using in its forecasts.

Regulatory Hurdles

While the North American market is considered low-risk, there are still shifting regulatory conditions that have the ability to derail expansion plans and impact the lifespan of mines. A clear danger in this regard is the risk of permitting delays at brownfield exploration sites because it could easily stall the development of productive new ore sources. Without new sources of ore in development, it could be tough to sustain the company’s production in the coming years.

Conclusion

Coeur’s change into a major player in North America is undeniable. But, it still has a complex path towards its 2026 target that is full of moving parts. While there are certainly execution hurdles, the combined power of seven high-quality operations gives it the resources to achieve its lofty goals. Ultimately, Coeur offers the opportunity of aggressive growth in stable jurisdictions; these attributes make it a strong bullish play for the precious metal rally.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Financials Report This Week; Expectations Are Quite High And Compares Are Tough
data.description:
This blog post will focus on JPMorgan (JPM) and Citigroup (C), with other earnings previews to follow.

Depending upon which source you use, there are either 14 or 18 financial sector companies reporting this coming. LSEG, in their “This Week in Earnings” report, noted on Friday that there are just 14 companies reporting this week in the entirety.

The point being, of the companies that will report this week, the vast majority will be banks and financials.

Tuesday morning, January 13th, investors will get a look at how JPMorgan performed in Q4 ’25, and then on Wednesday morning, January 14th, investors will hear from Citigroup, Bank of America (BAC) and Wells Fargo (WFC) - all of these institutions reporting before the market opens.

The backdrop for Q4 ’25 earnings reports is that, just one year ago, President Trump was elected to his second term, and the stock market responded nicely to the expectation for the financial sector of lower capital requirements, not to mention lower overall tax rates and regulations, as well as improved business conditions.

Major banks reported these year-over-year (y-o-y) growth rates for Q4 ’24 in early 2025:

  • JPMorgan - 11% net revenue growth and 58% EPS growth with an 11% EPS upside surprise;
  • Citi - 12% net revenue growth and 60% EPS growth with a 10% EPS upside surprise;
  • Bank of America - 11% net revenue growth and 31% EPS growth with a 12% EPS upside surprise;

(This blog does not model or track Wells Fargo financials).

For the financial sector as a whole, per the LSEG data, here’s how the financial sector performed in terms of y-o-y growth since Q4 ’24:

  • Q3 ’25 - +25% EPS growth and +8.1% revenue growth
  • Q2 ’25 - +14.2% EPS growth and +4% revenue growth
  • Q1 ’25 - +5.4% EPS growth and +3% revenue growth
  • Q4 ’24 - +35.1% EPS growth and +7.1% revenue growth

For Q4 ’11 (the first quarter this blog started tracking sector historical growth rates) to Q4 ’19, the financial sector “averaged” +12.9% EPS growth and +3.5% revenue growth.

JPMorgan

When JPM reports Tuesday morning before the opening bell, the “House that Jamie Built” should report a pretty good quarter, with sell-side analyst consensus expecting $4.95 in earnings per share (EPS) on $46.2 billion in net revenue for expected y-o-y growth of 3% and 8% respectively. If the sell-side consensus estimates are met exactly, JPM’s full-year 2025 EPS and revenue growth +10% and +3% respectively.

Note the rather modest y-o-y growth rates.

Strong “upside surprises” or beat rates are expected for the banking giant, as JPM is that almost perfect combination of capital markets, consumer, and commercial bank segments.

The above spreadsheet, taken from this blog’s own work, shows the percentage distribution of JPM by segment.

JPM’s capital markets group (Corp and Investment Banking) is almost half of net revenue and net income as of today. That’s not necessarily a bad thing in a capital markets environment like we are in now, supported by Administration policies, but that segment of the business is more capital market sensitive, and thus subject to sharper swings in net revenue and net income.

In Q3 ’25, JPM printed another quarter of +20% ROTCE (return on tangible common equity) on net revenue growth of +7%, and EPS growth of +16%. Corporate loan growth was just 7% y-o-y, and loan growth has been rather subdued during the last few years of economic growth.

JPM valuation

JPM is considered one of the most expensive of the major banks, primarily thanks to the tangible book valuation (TBV) of 2.3x TBV as of the last earnings report. However, this is offset by the +20% ROTCE, which is the highest of the major financial center banks. The closest major bank to JPM in terms of ROE/ROTCE is Bank of America, and that is mid-teens (of the banks this blog follows).

Trading at $329-330 per share and at 16x P/E, given expected 5% and 9% EPS growth in ’26 and ’27, JPM is reasonably valued on a P/E basis.

With the lower capital constraints on the banks, which Jamie Dimon railed against as far back as last decade, JPM has reduced their fully diluted share count by 2-3% every year, which is a nice plus for shareholders. Plus, Jamie has been increasing the JPM dividend of late, from $1.05 per quarter as of late 2023 to $1.50 as of Q4 ’25. It might get hiked again too.

Morningstar has a fair value estimate on JPMorgan of $259, while this blog’s valuation model estimates JPM’s fair value closer to $367. So, what I typically do is take the average of the two models - and that average is $313 per share.

To sum up JPM in one sentence, the bank is perfectly positioned within both the economic and political framework that we are faced with in the middle part of this decade. The stock is fairly valued to modestly overvalued on the metrics, and has momentum aspects to how it has traded in the last 18 months. Still, it’s been a big winner for clients and all shareholders, up 36.74% in calendar 2025.

Citigroup

When Citigroup reports their Q4 ’25 financial results on Wednesday morning, January 14th, 2026, consensus expectations are looking for $20.6 billion in net revenue growth to generate EPS of $1.67 for expected y-o-y growth of 5% and 25%. If consensus estimates are met for calendar 2025, Citi will have grown net revenue and EPS +3% and +25% respectively.

Like a lot of big banks, Citi’s capital markets group came through. The segment is now 25% of total Citi net revenue but generated 42% of its operating income, per the data that accompanied earnings. The bank is late to the asset gathering and wealth management game, although that looks like it’s starting to change.

Jayne Frasier has done a good job turning the bank around, but the ROTCE of +9% (compare that to JPM) probably implies there is more opportunity to reduce overhead and deploy capital better. After the headcount had been reduced from 240,000 in mid-2023, Citi’s current headcount is roughly 227,000. That could probably come down some, or perhaps be better aligned with some of the growth areas of the bank.

Citigroup valuation

Trading at $121 per share, Citi is now trading comfortably above both book value ($108.41 per share as of Q3 ’25) and tangible book value (TBV) of $95.72.

A big selling point around Citi’s valuation was that large financial center banks under book value (let alone tangible book value) were rare and hard to find, but exiting the extreme dilution in 2008 and 2009, and the reverse-splitting the stock in May 2011, left Citi in no-man’s land where bigger banks were generating faster EPS and revenue growth. Citi’s stock languished in the $40s and low $50s from 2013 through 2018, and then when COVID ended, the stock dropped back into this previous trading range from its multi-year high around $80 per share.

The low ROE / ROTCE is an issue, but I see Citi as a mirror image of JPM, even as Citi like JPM is now returning capital to shareholders in the form of dividends and share repurchases.

Morningstar’s fair value estimate on Citi is $90, while this blog’s model values it at closer to $100, so the average is $95.

The stock’s total return in calendar ’25 was a whopping 69.07%.

Summary / conclusion

With the financial sector performing the way it has, it’s hard to be even a little bit bearish on the group given the state of the economy and the likelihood that consumer and commercial credit metrics will remain in good shape.

Both JPMorgan and Citi have acted “momentumy” in the last few months, with the tailwinds continuing to be favorable for the sector.

Investors could make a good case that there is probably more upside for Citi over the next few years, given the events around 2008 and what happened to the stock in the aftermath. Plus, Jayne Frazier seems committed to re-positioning the bank’s businesses to reflect Citi's move more towards the finance center business model that the other banks have developed.

Is Wall Street nearing “peak financial conditions”? Hard to say. It’s looking like 2026 will be another reasonable year for both the economy and the markets.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All EPS and revenue estimates data is sourced from LSEG I/B/E/S.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fiserv: Beyond The Mirage, A Valuation Under More Realistic Assumptions
data.description:
Fiserv (FISV) operates a robust business model in the financial infrastructure and merchant acceptance segments. The stock collapsed last year from overvalued levels sustained by a CEO-driven narrative shaped by misaligned incentives. As that narrative unraveled, a new management team was tasked with implementing a turnaround plan to heal the self-inflicted damage. The shares now appear fairly valued at current levels, offering no margin of safety. Consequently, I assign a Hold rating to the stock. Finally, the Fiserv case offers essential lessons for investors that should be carefully considered before committing capital to any investment.

A Robust Business Model

Fiserv is a leader in the financial services industry, holding a unique position at the intersection of technology infrastructure for financial institutions and the merchant acceptance businesses.

In the former, Fiserv has a strong competitive position, particularly in the credit union segment, operating in a moderately concentrated market with high switching costs. Most financial institutions do not develop all of their operating systems in-house; rather, they rely on companies like Fiserv. The company provides licenses for these operating systems and is responsible for updating them as regulations evolve. Furthermore, it develops upgrades that allow institutions to incorporate new products, services, and capabilities, while also assisting in the migration of legacy systems to the cloud.

Merchant acceptance constitutes the other half of the firm's revenue, and it has been the primary growth component of its cash-generating machine. Through its Clover POS system, Fiserv allows its SMB clients to process their payments and provides them with a bundle of SaaS tools to help manage their businesses.

Large enterprise clients can use the Carat platform to orchestrate payments across multiple payment methods, acquirers, wallets, and channels through a unified interface.

In my view, this business model presents some characteristics that make it particularly attractive. First, it is highly profitable and converts a large proportion of its revenue into cash flow, as evidenced by EBIT and free cash flow margins of 28.7% and 24.7%, respectively, for the 2024 fiscal year. Second, while not perfectly hedged, it is an inflation-resilient business due to transaction-based fees over gross processing value in the merchant acquiring business. Third, operating leverage allows Fiserv to expand margins as it grows. Fourth, while 85% of its revenue is generated in the US and Canada, the firm operates in more than 100 countries, making international expansion an opportunity for growth in the years to come. Finally, the company has an extensive and diverse distribution network that enables it to acquire small, innovative firms, integrate their services into its existing platforms, and reach its broad client base. This can add an extra layer of growth on top of the organic rate, generating supplemental cash flow that can be used for reinvestment, additional M&A, or returned to shareholders via share buybacks.

On a negative note, the company operates in a highly regulated environment and faces significant operational and security risks. In addition, Fiserv relies on partnerships with banks and card associations, such as Visa and Mastercard, which can have significant bargaining power over the company.

While the digitalization of banking and the shift toward cashless payments are secular trends that have benefited Fiserv, growth for the coming years is expected to slow down even for the merchant acceptance business. Although integrated SaaS solutions will be a growth lever in the future, the firm must compete with niche players that are gaining traction in the market. Toast, a POS provider for the restaurant industry, is a clear example.

A Year of Value Destruction and Broken Narratives

2025 was terrible for Fiserv and will be remembered by shareholders as a period of massive value destruction, with the stock plummeting 67% in the year. This collapse is particularly striking for a company with the characteristics discussed so far, particularly for one that had been able to grow adjusted EPS at double digits for 39 consecutive years.

2024 concluded on a high note. Fiserv had announced several international expansion initiatives, partnerships, and the rollout of new vertical-specific Clover solutions. In December, former CEO Frank Bisignano was nominated to serve as Commissioner of the Social Security Administration, but the transition seemed orderly as Mike Lyons was designated in a few days. At that moment, the outlook was bullish; management's 2025 guidance (10-12% organic revenue growth and 15-17%) sent the stock soaring past $200.

Bisignano officially departed the company in May. While his tenure included some accomplishments - most notably the First Data integration, which fared better than the troubled FIS/Worldpay merger - his exit was not without controversy. Between mid-May and June, Bisignano sold $530 million worth of company stock. The sale itself was not the concern. U.S. regulations require incoming government officials to divest holdings that could create conflicts of interest, allowing for tax deferral. The issue was benefiting from his exceptionally optimistic forward guidance and share repurchases at high prices.

When Fiserv's new management team took over, they refined full-year guidance toward the lower end of the original range with the explanation centered on delays in the implementation of new initiatives. The Q3 earnings call presentation revealed that there was something more serious below the surface.

The Argentina Mirage: A Reality Check on Organic Growth

The reported double-digit organic growth rate was largely an illusion. Fiserv's calculations include figures tied to macro variables of Argentina, a hyperinflationary economy. While this exposure represents a small portion of the overall business, its impact was outsized: high nominal revenue in pesos, driven by soaring inflation and interest rates, was converted into US dollars at an overvalued regulated official exchange rate. This accounting effect created the appearance of robust dollar-denominated growth, which vanished as the trend in the variables began to revert.

Adding to the confusion, only items with a positive impact were taking part of organic revenue. The FX impact was excluded, as it was uncorrelated to inflation and interest rate dynamics.

Source: Q4 2024 earnings presentation

When Mike Lyons reframed the discussion by isolating the Argentina operation, investors were confronted with the reality that Fiserv's core business has been growing at mid-single digits for at least the past two years.

The Cost of Short-Termism

Although the guidance accounted for a reduction in macro tailwinds, it relied on new initiatives coming online in the back half of the year to bridge the gap. However, poor performance in Q3 slashed those expectations. Organic growth came in at just 1%, with a 3% decline in Financial Solutions; operating margin declined by 320 bps, and adjusted EPS was down 11% year-over-year. These results left management with no choice but to reset the guidance.

Q2 Guidance for FY 2025

Q3 Guidance for FY 2025

Organic revenue growth

10%

3.5% - 4%

Adjusted EPS

$10.15 - $10.3 (15-17% YoY)

$8.5 - $8.6

Adjusted operating margin

+ 100 bps

- 200 bps

Free cash flow

$5.5 B

$4.25 B

The discussion around these results in the last earnings call made it clear that much of the damage was self-inflicted. In essence, the focus had been on meeting quarterly targets at the expense of the long term. The company went too far in cutting costs and postponing investments, which has dampened its ability to grow. Also, actions such as imposing extra fees on Clover users at the end of 2024 negatively impacted client satisfaction. Capital allocation has also proven to be poor, destroying value through share repurchases executed at overvalued prices and in amounts that exceeded Fiserv's cash-generating capacity. This pushed leverage close to the upper end of management's stated range and, at the same time, constrained growth.

Taking the analysis a step further, we could argue that this excessive short-termism may have been fueled by misaligned incentives. While this may only appear obvious ex post, management compensation tied to metrics such as stock price, organic revenue, and adjusted EPS growth likely played a role. In addition, the Board may not have been effective in challenging business practices or overly optimistic guidance, particularly given that Bisignano was wearing both hats as Chairman and CEO. Finally, his nomination to lead the Social Security Administration, together with the resulting obligation to divest his equity positions, may have acted as an accelerator of trends that were already in place.

Continuing with this line of reasoning, one could argue that incoming CEOs have incentives to paint a dire picture and set a lower bar, allowing for easier beats in the future. In my view, while this is a possibility, it doesn't change the fact that the company's challenges are significant and very real.

The Turnaround Plan

Given his background in traditional banking, Mike Lyons's profile appears far from that of a disruptive or visionary CEO. Rather, he is best characterized as an operator, focused on execution within a highly complex and regulated business, with broad experience in M&A. This profile appears well suited to the task at hand, and his One Fiserv action plan looks adequate to address the challenges discussed so far. The plan consists of the following:

  • Shift in mindset from short-term focus toward client satisfaction. This approach is clearly aligned with long-term value creation, but it comes with near-term trade-offs in the form of lower margins and higher investment requirements. Actions such as reversing extra fees on Clover users and streamlining the core banking systems offering from 16 platforms down to 5 are consistent with this renewed focus.
  • A refresh of the leadership team. New presidents were appointed for each of the two divisions, there is also a new CFO, and three independent directors, including the chairman.
  • A more disciplined approach to capital allocation, alongside the divestment of non-core businesses.
  • Additional initiatives, including improved communication with investors, enhanced forecasting capabilities, and the incorporation of AI and stablecoin technologies.

Management clearly got off on the wrong foot at the start of its tenure, as it made a mistake in the Q2 guidance recalibration. Given the time Mike Lyons had already spent at the company, he should have had a better grasp of both the reversal in Argentina's macroeconomic trends and the core business's limited ability to offset that impact. That said, publicly admitting this mistake and being willing to accept short-term pain are signs of a good character, but he probably had no other choice.

In my view, the turnaround plan seems adequate at a high level, but leadership has burned through much of its credibility, with no further room for negative surprises. While the next quarter will likely be a continuation, the specifics laid down in the upcoming Investor Day should demonstrate whether the company is truly moving in the right direction for the years ahead.

Valuation

My investment thesis is that the turnaround plan has a reasonable probability of success. This conviction is driven by the quality of the firm's assets and its strategic position within the financial services ecosystem.

The team tasked with execution seems competent, but they are still new to Fiserv. This is a complex organization with many moving parts, so my expectation is that the implementation will take some time and should come with the need for higher investments and margin compression in the short term.

In my view, the post-Covid glory days of the payments industry are now behind us, and Fiserv has reached a scale where its size is playing against it in terms of growth. Still, the space remains highly dynamic, with competitors continuing to innovate, which means the company must be careful not to fall behind while it works through its internal challenges.

To account for these dynamics, my valuation is based on a three-stage DCF model. A two-year turnaround period is followed by eight years of moderate growth and a terminal value representing long-term steady-state growth. Other main assumptions are outlined below.

Litigation risk is a tangible threat for this company. Considering the controversial actions of the former CEO, his high public profile, and the ongoing scrutiny from Congress, this risk should not be dismissed. While I don't claim legal expertise, I believe that in financial markets it is not reasonable to hide from risk but rather to confront it using the tools at hand. Based on historical benchmarking of shareholder class actions involving material harm, I have baked a $1.5 billion litigation liability into my model.

The model yields an intrinsic value of $65 per share. Since the stock is currently trading near $70, it appears fairly valued, and I'm remaining on the sidelines. I would consider going long only if 'Mr. Market' offers this name at a wider discount. For the time being, I believe there are more compelling opportunities elsewhere.

Your Takeaway: A Few Lessons for Investors

Whether you decide to invest in Fiserv or not, I think this story offers several lessons that can help us become better investors:

First, incentives always matter. We should not automatically assume that managers will act in shareholders' best interests. In practice, decision-making is heavily influenced by what executives are rewarded for.

Second, always do your own due diligence. As investors, we are often tempted to rely on company guidance or analysts' forecasts under the premise that they have better information. While this might be true in most cases, we should recognize that they have their own incentives and biases.

Third, long-term focus and disciplined capital allocation are critical to value creation. Stock buybacks, for instance, can destroy wealth when executed at rich valuations, even when they are presented as a shareholder-friendly decision.

Fourth, consistent growth above industry averages and high margins is a rare combination. When faced with such a situation, investors should assess whether the underlying drivers for that performance are sustainable.

Ultimately, even great businesses with established moats can find themselves in a difficult position if they are poorly managed.

data.title: Coinbase: Long-Term Thesis Intact Ahead Of Q4
data.description:
Coinbase (COIN) is a major crypto exchange that I've covered a few times. The stock is down from the highs it saw in 2025. I believe this decline is short-term in nature. The company's long-term focus should serve it and shareholders well.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I wrote about COIN last, it was May 2025, and Q2 was still ongoing.

I talked about how transaction revenue was volatile, prone to cyclical factors. I also pointed out how COIN had been leveraging their exchange business to form a growing core of subscription-based revenue, thanks in large part to their stablecoin product through their alliance with Circle Internet Group (CRCL).

Wanting more financial data for 2025, I declined to update my Discounted Cash Flow calculation, giving it a fair value of $366 per share for a 10% discount rate. Because the margin of safety was large for what I felt was a grower, I rated it a Buy.

Updates Since Last May

Numerous things have happened since I wrote in May, which both drove the stock up and then back down.

First was the passage of the GENIUS Act, the first major federal policy on crypto, which specifically provided a framework for stablecoins, one of COIN's key products. Secondly, the crypto market took a big hit starting on Oct. 10. A new tariff announcement by President Trump caused the crypto market to pull down, which in turn forced liquidations of heavily leveraged accounts. General nervousness by the market (crypto and stocks) continued to bring it down.

Looking at the price of Bitcoin (BTC-USD), we can see that the flurry of good news (as the GENIUS Act brought more people and institutions into crypto). This brought BTC to a high of $123K right before Oct. 10. The bearishness that followed brought it back under $100K again, and it has yet to recover fully.

COIN has a similar story, but its ATH was reached in July at $420, and this coincided more closely with the GENIUS Act. While there's some correlation with BTC, it's also clear that what benefits COIN's earnings is a consideration here. Let's talk a bit about those.

While the transaction part of their revenue has grown, the stablecoin product has had the most consistent growth across quarters, with the GENIUS Act supporting an ATH of $354M in revenue from this product. What about the impact on cash flows?

A straight look at the cash flow statement shows a surprising change, negative operating cash flow! Nevertheless, we see that the biggest driver here was $2.5B in net changes in operating assets and liabilities.

A closer look shows that nearly all of this is a result of growth in their USDC product. An increase in USDC balance follows the spike in adoption. As Circle and Coinbase gain yield from the short-term interest of the real cash behind the USDC, it is considered an operating asset, and gains in this position result in a decline.

As seen above, the market cap of USDC has grown from about $45B a year ago to $75B more recently. It has sagged less from the bearishness in the broader crypto market. Pegged to USD 1:1, growth purely reflects how the product is gaining adoption.

If we adjust for the impact of USDC balances, operating cash flow for the first nine months of 2025 comes to $1,803M. Annualized, this is about $2,404M.

COIN did not report meaningful capex in the usual sense for these nine months. I did highlight fiat loan originations in the table above because this is an interesting item. The only thing we might consider capex is the cash portion of the acquisition of Deribit.

This was a $4.3B deal, of which about $3.6B was done in shares of COIN. Fully diluted, COIN's total shares outstanding are now 292M. The reason for this dilutive acquisition is simple. In their Q3 shareholder letter, management cited crypto derivatives as accounting for 80% of crypto trading volume, and Deribit is the largest exchange in crypto derivatives, when measured by trading volume and open interest.

Looking at the balance sheet, we see a very healthy company coming out of the third quarter. Their $8.7B in cash is sufficient to support their $7.3B in total debt, and USDC balances are quickly becoming one of their largest operating assets overall.

Outlook and Valuation

COIN trades back in a range where I first considered it a Buy again, $240 per share for a market cap of $66B. Let's examine whether or not I still consider it a Buy.

First, consider the value proposition. Annualized adjusted OCF is $2,404M. With M&A as the only likely form of capex for their capital-light business, it takes some guess work, but we could imagine that this is a business that produces about $2B in adjusted FCF currently. Based on the fully diluted shares outstanding, that would be $6.85 per share.

Let's compare some growth assumptions with Discounted Cash Flow models that utilize this adjusted FCF per share.

On the left, I assumed a long-term CAGR of 15% with a similar terminal multiple. On the right, I only raised the CAGR to 20%, while keeping the terminal multiple at 15, mainly to illustrate how a marginal difference in growth can add up. Both use 10% discount rates to compare to market indices, suggesting fair values of $249 and $359, respectively.

In general, I think there is plenty of reason to think that long-term growth will occur, given the framework provided by the GENIUS Act. This has created more stability and peace of mind for new businesses to join in, hence the large growth of COIN's USDC balance this year, even in spite of the broader bearishness of crypto since October.

While transaction revenue from trading on their exchange fluctuates each quarter, I suspect it should generally grow as well, particularly as they work to become an "everything exchange," something mentioned heavily in Q3 earnings. CEO Brian Armstrong said this in the earnings call:

In Q2, we introduced the Everything Exchange, a one-stop shop to trade every asset class. Customers want one venue to trade spot crypto assets, derivatives and options, but also equities, prediction markets, commodities and more. In Q3, we executed on that vision by expanding spot coverage, growing our derivatives offering and laying the groundwork for new asset classes on our platform.

This largely explains the acquisition of Deribit and likely hints at acquisitions here and there to come.

I think a big source of growth in their stablecoin product still lies ahead, as an alternative to merchant services. CFO Alesia Haas said in the call:

But we're also seeing small and medium-sized businesses really come to our platform as we enable them to more efficiently manage their capital and their liquidity through instant settlement via stablecoins, while we're earning rewards now on any idle funds that they hold in USDC. So we've seen great early traction with over 1,000 businesses onboarded, and we have a growing wait list.

As stablecoins are very nearly a zero-cost merchant service, I expect room for this to grow beyond the role they occupy as a placeholder of value for crypto trading. It starts with B2B transactions in order to lower costs. It steadily can become more adopted, for more ordinary uses by individuals.

The reach of the exchange is growing. The balance of the USDC product is growing. One's revenue is volatile; the other's is stable, but both lines are showing signs of growth.

Additionally, Coinbase is finding good opportunities to create revenue. For example, they have been a source of financing for other crypto players by creating crypto-backed credit facilities for them. When I wrote about CleanSpark (CLSK) recently, they spoke about a $200M line with them. There are spots where the COIN can leverage its leadership and relationships to produce strong yield.

Risk Factors

Both valuations assume some growth, and either one can be weakened by setbacks that may be out of COIN's control. The bearishness in Q4 is very likely to have impacted trading volume, and so there could be a hit in full-year results from this.

Meanwhile, rate cuts by the Federal Reserve began in September, and two more followed before the end of 2025. Both of these trends will likely make Q4 look weaker. Continued rate cuts into 2026 could make growth flat, even if USDC balances grow, as short-term interest rates are what make the balance an operating asset at all.

Additionally, stock-based compensation remains significant, over $600M in the first nine months of 2025. If this continues, there's a dilutive trend that may lower the long-term CAGR of FCF per share by a few percent.

While there aren't immediately threatening risk factors, owing to their capital-light model and low use of leverage, COIN is priced for at least a little growth, and so investors depend on that in order to come out ahead over time.

Conclusion

Everything taken into account, I think setbacks like Q4's bearishness are temporary in nature. Coinbase is not anchoring itself to being a crypto exchange; it works to become a more universal fintech with durable revenue sources. For that reason, I think shareholders can get a fair value for moderate execution but undervaluation for excellent execution. For that reason, I maintain my Buy rating today.

data.title: Dividend Income: Lanny's November 2025 Summary
data.description:
This is what dividend investing is all about! Investing in dividend stocks allows you to earn dividend income, the best passive income stream! Bias, you better believe it.

Time to dive into Lanny’s November 2025 dividend income results! Were records set? Almost to financial freedom? One day and one month at a time!

Dividend Income

Dividend income is the fruit from the labor of investing your money in the stock market. Further, dividend income is my primary vehicle on the road to financial freedom.

How do I research & screen for dividend stocks prior to making a purchase? I use our Dividend Diplomat Stock Screener and trade on SoFi’s investment platform.

I also automatically invest and max out, pre-tax, my 401(k) through work and my Health Savings Account. This allows me to save a ton of money on taxes (aka thousands), which allows me to invest even more. In addition, all dividends I receive are automatically being reinvested back into the company that paid the dividend, aka Dividend Reinvestment Plan or DRIP for short. This takes the emotion out of timing the market and builds onto my passive income stream!

Growing your dividend income takes time and consistency. Investing as often, and early, as you can allows compound interest (aka dividends) to work its magic. I have gone from making $2.70 in a single month in dividend income to well over… $10,000+ in a single month. My dividend income record was set in December of 2021. Was it broken this month? The power of compounding and dividend reinvestment is a wonderful component to the portfolio. Each and every month, whether big or small, I continue to report the passive income that dividend investing provides me. Why?

*Not pictured is my wife’s dividend income above*

I want to show you that dividend investing makes it possible to achieve financial freedom and/or financial independence. We all start somewhere, but consistently investing, compounding (reinvesting) dividends and keeping it simple allows you to be in a significantly better position than most. Further, if I can grow this portfolio and income stream, you can too.

Dividend Income – November 2025

Now, on to the numbers… In November, we (my wife and I) received a dividend income total of $1,489.90. Another $1,000+. Keeping the streak alive! Though I’d really like $2,000…

The amount and number of stocks listed below show you what it means to buy and hold for the long term. Most of the positions I have owned for years, letting dividend growth and reinvestment do its thing. This is what dividend investing for financial freedom is all about. The passive income stream is growing at a rapid pace.

2023 was up 24%. Guess what? 2024 was up 23%! 2025 was also up over 16%. Can we get 4 years in a row?! Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has started the year in the $90,000 range, two interest rate cuts are priced in the market, and the Fed has started a mini quantitative easing.

Here is the breakdown of dividend income for the month between taxable and retirement (far-right column, under “Retirement”) accounts. In addition, “W” means my wife’s account:

Nothing really new up here. MAIN is probably the latest addition, but even that stock was quite a few years ago.

Therefore, all the dividends are pretty boring companies, but at least Procter & Gamble (PG) is closing in on the $100 threshold for me! Well, combined with my wife, we are almost at $130!

I also split out my retirement accounts in the far-right column, and the taxable account dividends are in the left two columns. The retirement accounts are composed of HSA investments, Roth and traditional IRAs, as well as our work 401(k) accounts. In total, the retirement accounts brought in a total dividend income amount of $446.54, or 30% of the dividend income total. Stacking income, no matter what. Let's go.

Dividend Income Year over Year Comparison

2024:

2025:

Our dividend income went up $50, let’s call it. Not a huge amount, but it’s an increase.

The dividends each company is paying me grew. In fact, we had less positions, as we exited Hormel (HRL) and Reynolds American (REYN)); we haven’t looked back.

Further, I even exited Citizens & Northern (CZNC) in my taxable account.

Therefore, this is purely dividend growth and reinvestment.

How did your November dividend income compare?

Dividend Increases

We had 4 dividend increases, which is awesome. Which one was the surprising dividend increase? Easy here…

Capital One Financial (COF). After they acquired Discover, I was not sure what to expect. Well… a 33% dividend increase was huge.

Lastly, Emerson (EMR) at 5% was much higher than expected, with Aflac (AFL) being much lower than expected with their 5%. This was the year of 5%.

In total, dividend increases created $88.11 in additional passive dividend income. I would need to invest $2,517 at a 3.50% dividend yield in order to add that income. Thank you for the increases, as I didn’t have to come up with the capital to create that form of income!

Dividend Income Conclusion & Summary

The name of the game is to apply what you learn through financial education. The next steps are to maximize every dollar for investment opportunities and live life on your own terms. Therefore, my plan is to demonstrate that dividend income can be a revenue engine. A revenue engine that allows you to take back control of your life. A revenue engine to help you reach financial freedom. Dividend investing, once you learn the right way, becomes easier and starts to immensely make sense!

Excited for the future, no doubt. Furthermore, all of the investing from last year and moves this year show that my aim to save 60% of my income and making every dollar count has provided the dividend growth.
