data.title: Energy Fuels: Not For Investors
data.description:
The problem with stocks that do not have earnings, like Energy Fuels (UUUU) is that most valuation models mentioned in the finance world are based upon some projection of cash flow back to investors to give that entity a value. Oftentimes, a company with no earnings has this great story about how the business will generate a ton of cash. But so many times in my experience, it is worth the wait to get to the point where there will be earnings. The reason for this is that the market often re-evaluates the stock (usually downwards) to come up with a realistic cash flow trend into the future once the business actually gets going. While economists and finance people (who are a subset of economics) assume perfect information, a stock with no earnings has nearly anything but perfect information. That makes valuations suspect until the information about the earnings the business will generate is much more tangible.

Energy Fuels claims to have a core uranium business. Mining companies, like oil & gas, tend to attract investors with the promise of big profits. But few seldom deliver. Keep in mind that a company that is raising cash in public like this one is had the chance to raise that money with private investors (and then go public at a much later stage). But private money essentially turned this opportunity down. Otherwise, this company would not be public before it is closer to having an actual business with cash generation.

Note that the common stock price already has a market value of 4 billion. To justify a common stock price like that, the company would need to generate free cash flow of about four hundred million dollars. But as of right now, the company is generating nothing close to that. That allows for a lot of downside potential in the event of a disappointment. In fact, it could well cost the investor the total value of the investment because a company that is losing money can lose more money.

Management Statements

Understand that a company like this that has to raise money within 12 months can easily trigger a "going concern" statement that very much would affect the market value of the stock price. Therefore, smart managements make sure that enough resources are available so that a "going concern" statement is not a mandatory part of the financial statements. This is one of many reasons that investors should check the official filings of a company like this for whatever statement that management makes about the situation.

If an investor goes to the financials section of the website and clicks on the 10-Q, it takes some time to get to this particular section because the further away from the front of the 10-Q this is, the better.

This is essentially a step up from the going concern statement. But combined with the other warnings, the investor is being told that this company needs some help from the pricing environment to succeed. Now, that is not exactly advanced knowledge. But it is important to note that if there is a setback in breakeven projections, it could prove either fatal to the company or a material change to the future value of the business. This is a huge reason to wait for actual earnings before jumping into something like this.

Otherwise, this is the storied "musical chairs game- Wall Street Style" and the investor risks not having a chair. I don't know how many times over the years I have seen a favorable report generated on a company like this because a big seller wanted out and the brokerage figured that a favorable report would generate the interest to get the sale done.

Then, if and when earnings actually happened, the stock price was often 50% or more lower. But what really happens here is the lottery ticket effect, in that one big winner has got everybody willing to accept those losses for that one winner. Good luck with that strategy.

Finances

This company issued a convertible bond. That often puts a lid on the capital appreciation that so many count on to make this a viable investment idea in the first place.

Note that this is the mining business (basically). There is more than a fair amount of development and exploration underway when the market value of the stock is in the billions. It is highly likely (therefore), in my opinion, that cash flow to justify that market value, if it ever happens, is years away.

That likely means that this is not the last cash raise project. That $700 million is a decent dilution of the valuation of the market value of the stock even before the terms are reviewed.

More importantly, the conversion of that bond is something that I have not often seen. It often takes quite a story that captures the imagination for that convertible to convert. Most of the time, what seems to happen, in my opinion, is that a lid is put on the share price somewhere in the vicinity of the conversion value. But that seriously detracts from that future of a "homerun" share price.

The current situation shows nothing close to a business size that would come anywhere close to supporting the current stock price. While the growth rate is impressive, it is from a very low base. Plus, there is an old saying on Wall Street that applies here: "Do not invest in a company losing money because it may lose more money".

Cash Flow Statement

This company is going through cash like water. As some of the other projects ramp up, there is going to be cash needed for them as well. But as the current statement will show, the cash burn rate is already increasing.

As shown above, the company is very likely to continue raising cash for the foreseeable future. As other projects ramp up, the cash needs will change. The big question is if any cash-generating activities will help out at any time in the future in a material fashion.

Right now, I would wait for that to happen. There is definitely no rush to "jump in" given what has been shown so far.

Stockholders Equity Statement

It should surprise no one that there is already some dilution ongoing through the payment of some items in shares of stock.

Understand that the shares used so far in this fashion are not that material when compared to the convertible debt offering. However, the existence of shares "leaking out" in this fashion indicates a certain level of cash need that shareholders need to be aware of. Some may think that the convertible offering is large enough to put an end to this. However, my own experience would be that it is not necessarily the case. This is something that definitely needs monitoring, as it is another form of dilution rarely discussed by management. Over time, it can be extremely meaningful.

The Business

This is the mining business.

There is a lot going on here. Anyone who thinks that this is somehow going to miraculously turn into a viable business in the next five years really needs to rethink the situation. Some of these are big and really just getting started. Anything that does produce cash is going to see that cash reinvested in the business for some time to come. There may be value here, but in my opinion that traditional going business that throws off cash to shareholders is quite a few years away.

I can hope that management does better. But hope is not an investment strategy.

Summary

Energy Fuels is a strong sell idea. I have no idea how to value the stock based upon current prospects. Nor do I know if there will be a viable business down the road that justifies the current stock price. Therefore, I get out the popcorn and soda and watch from the sidelines.

Companies like these may never make it all the way to a going concern with decent cash flow. So, there is no reason for me to take the risk. Furthermore, a company that sells convertible securities has huge odds in favor of a stock price that is 50% or more lower at least once in the next roughly 18 months. David Dreman covers this in his "Contrarian Investment" series of books. But even without that, it was a topic of discussion in finance class.

The odds of an investment in a company like this succeeding are very much against the investor. I therefore stay far away from it. I have enough challenges with making money from regular businesses, let alone a situation like this where I cannot tell if there will be a viable business down the road in the first place.

Risks

The projects can succeed to the point where the stock is undervalued at the current price. That very much goes against the odds of what usually happens in my experience.

Any of the projects underway can disappoint enough to send the stock price down considerably from where it is now.

Rapid growth has its own risks. Those risks include some daunting logistics as all these projects ramp up. Loss of quality control and other mistakes are a huge concern with rapid growth.

We currently have some political uncertainty marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs that could either impede or help the proposed business. A potential recession could impede future cash raises and imperil the business.

data.title: MarketWise: Trading At 5x FCF With Solid Top-Line Momentum
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Shares of MarketWise (MKTW) have performed strongly over the past year, rising by nearly 50%. I have remained bullish on the stock and still feel that its stock price has not caught up with the rapidly improving business performance and falling risk profile.

After several years of shrinking billings and compressing margins since 2021, 2025 is set to mark the first year of a return to growth alongside meaningful margin expansion. Preliminary guidance from MKTW's CEO for FY26 calls for billings of approximately $290 million, around 11% higher compared to FY25, representing a modest growth acceleration ahead. Moreover, FCF guidance for FY26 of $45 million is sharply higher than the $33 million I expect for FY25.

Based on these expectations, MKTW stock trades at just 5.4x this year's FCF. FY26 guidance, which was announced along with Q3 earnings in November, was much higher than my expectations. However, I consider these targets very achievable, especially as the business has outperformed guidance in the first three quarters of 2025. Yet the stock remains severely undervalued, with no debt on its balance sheet and a dividend yield of 12.5%.

I would point to the fact that MKTW has minimal analyst coverage and management holds no earnings calls. Additionally, given the low float, liquidity is limited with an average daily trading volume of roughly $200,000 over the past three months.

What makes the situation even more compelling is the recent acquisition offer of $17.25 per share from its largest shareholder, which serves as a potential catalyst and sets a floor for the stock. While the offer is still under review and may not result in a deal, the risk-reward is highly compelling, with upside of at least 50% to my fair value estimate. I am therefore upgrading MKTW from a Buy to a Strong Buy.

Strong 2025 with a Promising Outlook for 2026

Q3 Billings, which is the relevant metric to follow in the case of MKTW as it involves subscriptions that lead to revenue being recognized ratably, was $63.7 million, as shown above. The strong return to billings growth followed the lapping of the winddown of the Legacy Research business due to internal policy violations by management. Following this, there are currently 8 different investment research-related brands under the MKTW umbrella. Wide Moat Research and Brownstone Research are relatively newer brands demonstrating healthy growth, with the former run by Brad Thomas, together helping offset the loss from Legacy Research.

While Q1 billings spiked as a result of increased marketing spend, subsequent quarters have shown healthy year-over-year growth. With management guiding for Q4 billings of $67 million, FY25 billings is expected to land at $260 million, up 8.8% versus the prior year. This has been achieved despite the continued decline in paid subscribers, as shown below. Meanwhile, ARPU has grown sharply from $417 in Q3 2024 to $566 in Q3 2025, as management has intentionally focused on its high-value subscribers.

With annual capex and capitalized R&D costs amounting to only around $1 million, this business is highly FCF generative. With $20.7 million in FCF generated in the first three quarters of 2025, I expect full-year FCF of $33 million, which is well above management's guidance of $30 million.

Turning to 2026, management expects full-year billings of $290 million. I believe this is a realistic target, as Q4 billings are expected to exit 2025 with 20% growth, and the annualized Q4 run rate stands at $268 million. FCF of $45 million in FY26 implies a margin of 15.5%, expanding from 12.6% in FY25. This aligns with my previous analysis that normalized margins for this business should comfortably exceed 15%. MKTW has historically achieved FCF margins north of 20%, peaking at 27% in FY21. This points to meaningful margin expansion potential for the business in the coming years if modest billings growth is sustained.

MKTW Stock is Undervalued with Limited Downside

Based on management's preliminary FCF guidance of $45 million for FY26, at the current share price of $15.1, MKTW stock trades at a Price/FCF multiple of 5.4. This valuation is in line with an online content publisher like Ziff Davis (ZD), which I also cover with a Buy rating. ZD stock trades at close to 5x FCF and is demonstrating low single-digit organic growth. The depressed valuation assigned by the market, which I find unjustified, comes from the risk related to search traffic disruption and AI commoditizing content creation. While these risks may be overstated for ZD, they are even less significant for MKTW.

According to a prior investor presentation, MKTW uses a diverse set of channels for user acquisition, with limited reliance on search traffic. When it comes to content creation, while AI may lead to greater commoditization of basic research, MKTW’s team of editors brings years of experience and deep market expertise, providing insights that go beyond what AI and algorithms alone can deliver.

Being a subscription business, MKTW ought to trade in line with other subscription businesses with similar growth rates, such as The New York Times Company (NYT), which trades at over 20 times FCF. Given the higher churn rates associated with its subscriptions, I believe MKTW deserves to trade at a meaningful discount to NYT. With 11% billings growth expected ahead, even assuming a conservative FCF multiple of 8x, this implies a share price of $22.4, which represents an upside of close to 50%. Besides this, the higher FCF generation should allow for dividends to increase compared to the $1.9 paid out in FY25, which represents a yield of 12.5% currently.

The aspect that makes the investment case for MKTW more compelling is the recent unsolicited offer from its largest shareholder, Monument & Cathedral Holding, to acquire the shares that it doesn't own for $17.25 per share. Notably, they own nearly 43% of MKTW's total shares outstanding, and since Class A and Class B shares each carry one vote, Monument & Cathedral’s existing ownership gives them significant influence, but they would still need additional votes from other holders. While the offer allows for certain shareholders to retain that stake after MKTW is taken private, a key demand is the termination of the tax receivable agreement for these shareholders.

While the offer sets a floor for the share price, I believe it is too low and expect it to be raised. I would even prefer the company remain public, given the upside potential I see. While a special committee within MKTW's board is currently engaging with financial and legal advisors to review the offer, the outcome here remains uncertain. I do not see how they can reach a conclusion that this price is fair to all shareholders, including MKTW's founder Porter Stansberry, who has an 18.5% stake.

Given that the acquirer has stated their willingness to negotiate a mutually accepted transaction, I believe that there is a high likelihood that the offer price is raised by at least 20%. Even if this deal does not go through, the share price should eventually catch up with the growing FCF generated by the underlying business, thereby driving future investor returns.

Risks

As I have stated above, I do not view the acquisition failing as a material risk. Instead, I will focus on the key metrics that determine the health of the business. In this regard, I continue to look for quarterly net renewal billings to stay above $15 million. Renewal billings are significantly more profitable for MKTW than new marketing billings since they require no marketing costs. This declined to $14.1 million in Q3, pointing to higher churn within the existing customer base. I will look for this metric to perk up in the coming quarters as the worst of the decline in paid subscribers appears to be behind them. I will look for further stabilization in the number of paid subscribers at around the level seen in Q3, as I believe this will be a key driver for the market to regain confidence that the business is able to grow its subscriber base.

While macroeconomic conditions and market performance may affect investor sentiment toward financial research in the near term, the long-term performance of MKTW’s brands depends heavily on key personnel who conduct research and generate content. Any disruption in this area could significantly impact the parent company’s financial performance, as seen previously with the Legacy Research business.

MKTW Stock is a Strong Buy

Despite the solid performance in the first three quarters of 2025 and the strong outlook for top-line growth and FCF generation forecasted for 2026, shares continue to trade cheaply at just 5.4x FCF. Meanwhile, with no debt on its balance sheet, management is returning nearly all FCF to shareholders through dividends. Additionally, the $17.25 per share acquisition offer from its largest shareholder serves as a potential catalyst and provides strong downside support. Given this compelling risk-reward setup, I am upgrading MKTW stock to a Strong Buy.
